---
title: "8. Finding and Verifying Prior Evidence"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb02_research_builds_on_research_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Which sources count as *verified evidence* you may
> build on, and whether the gap you want to claim is genuinely open or just a
> search you stopped too early.

## Why this decision matters

Picture a thesis advisor handing your first draft back across the desk. "Show me
the conversation your work joins," she says, "not a pile of summaries. Which
studies agree, which fight, and where exactly is the hole you say you are
filling?" That question decides whether your project has a place to stand. Get it
wrong in one of two ways and the whole project tilts. Cite a source that does not
exist, or does not say what you claim, and every result resting on it is
compromised. Announce a gap that a paper already filled, and you have promised a
contribution you cannot deliver. This chapter equips you to make both calls and
defend them.

## The concept

A field is a **cumulative conversation**: a running argument where each study
answers, extends, or challenges the ones before it. For example, one lab reports
that a soil treatment raises crop yield, and the next lab tests whether the effect
holds in a different climate. Your job is not to invent an island. It is to find
the conversation your question belongs to and add one honest sentence to it.

Every source you keep passes through one habit. The **retrieval-verification
loop** is four steps: ask any tool to surface leads, retrieve the actual source
yourself, verify it exists and says what was claimed, and document where you found
it. A **lead** is a candidate source you have not confirmed yet, such as a
citation an AI hands you. A lead stays a lead until retrieval and verification
promote it to a source.

Two named failures are what the loop exists to catch. A **citation
hallucination** is an AI inventing a source that does not exist, dressed in a
real-sounding author pair, a recent year, and a plausible journal. A
**mischaracterized source** is a real paper cited for a claim it never makes, such
as a study that reports a correlation being cited as proof of a cause. A source
can be real and still be mischaracterized, so "does it exist" and "does it say
this" are two separate checks.

Once you hold one verified source, you grow the rest by **snowballing**: walking
its links in two directions. A **backward citation search** reads the source's own
reference list to find the older work it built on. A **forward citation search**
finds the newer works that cite it, through a "Cited by" trail. Two solid sources,
snowballed both ways, become ten.

That is also how you earn the word gap. A **genuine gap** is a question your
verified sources actually leave unanswered, confirmed by looking. An **unfinished
search** is a question you simply stopped chasing. They feel identical from your
desk, and only one is a real contribution. The test is retrieval: you have earned
the word gap only after real sources, followed forward and backward, still stay
silent on your question.

## A worked example

You are an honors student in agronomy asking whether adding **biochar** (charcoal
made from crop waste and mixed into soil) raises maize yield in sandy fields. You
ask an AI tool for evidence. It returns a confident citation: a named author pair,
a 2021 date, a real-sounding soil-science journal, and a tidy finding that biochar
lifted yield by 18 percent. Perfect. So you go to read it, and it is nowhere. Not
in the library portal, not on Google Scholar, not on the publisher's site.

Here is the decision on the table. Is the missing study proof that "no one has
tested biochar in sandy soil, so my gap is real"? No. Three things could be true.
The AI **fabricated** the study whole, and there is nothing to find. It **garbled**
a real source, right idea but wrong author or year, so you search the claim itself
and the likely real authors. Or the paper is real but **paywalled or obscure**,
reachable through the library or by emailing the author. Your next move is the same
in all three: the lead stays out of your verified column until retrieval succeeds.

So you do the work. You retrieve four real soil-science studies through the library
database, read each one, and snowball. Backward from one 2019 field trial you find
the older greenhouse study it built on. Forward through "Cited by" you find two
newer trials that tested the same idea in loam and clay. Now you can see the
conversation: biochar helps yield in some soils, the size varies, and none of the
four you retrieved ran the trial in the sandy soil your county farms. *That* quiet
spot, ringed by sources you opened yourself, is a gap you can defend.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb02 — Research builds on research: verified
evidence + real gaps**. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells
alongside this chapter. In the lab you run the retrieval-verification loop on your
own claim and watch a simulation collapse a tidy pile of fifteen AI citations down
to the handful that survive retrieval, then build an evidence map whose one empty
square is your gap.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each block below is something you
would actually type.

```text
My research question is: [paste it]. The claim I need sources for is:
[paste the claim]. List the five published studies most relevant to this
claim, each with authors, year, exact title, and venue, so I can retrieve
and verify each one myself.
```
*After running, verify:* search each exact title in quotes before believing it
exists. This counters **confident fabrication** (a real-sounding citation for a
paper that was never written).

```text
Here are the five sources I retrieved and read: [paste them]. For each, state
the single claim its evidence actually supports, and flag any where my
one-line summary reaches further than the study does.
```
*After running, verify:* read each flagged claim against the paper's own results
section. This counters the **mischaracterized source** and **silent scope change**
(a correlation quietly reported as a cause).

```text
Here is my research question and the gap I placed: [paste them]. Argue the gap
is NOT real: name up to three published studies that may already fill it, with
authors, year, title, and venue. Do not reassure me.
```
*After running, verify:* retrieve every study it names; a source you cannot open
closes nothing. This counters **sycophantic agreement** (a partner that praises
your gap has flattered you, not tested it).

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Two calls stay yours alone, because your name goes on the map. First, **whether a
source counts as verified**: you retrieved it, opened it, and read the claim
yourself, so no AI summary substitutes for the paper. Second, **whether your gap is
genuine**: you walked one backward and one forward link from each verified source
and the question still stands unanswered. An AI can point; only you can vouch.
:::

## An AI failure case

The failure to expect here is confident fabrication. Ask a general-purpose tool for
"the most-cited study on biochar and maize yield in sandy soil" and it will often
produce one, complete with a DOI-shaped string and a real journal name, because
producing a fluent citation is exactly what it is built to do. The detail is not
evidence of existence. You catch it the moment you try to retrieve it: paste the
exact title in quotation marks into the library database and Google Scholar. If
nothing resolves anywhere it should live, the citation is fabricated until proven
otherwise, and it never enters your ledger. The fluency was the trap; the empty
search result is the tell.

## Verification laboratory

The chapter's key result is your verified-source list, and the method that confirms
it is **primary-source reading** from the [verification
guide](../../ai_resources/verification_guide.md): for any citation, find the actual
source and read the relevant line. Reach for it whenever an output cites a study, a
statistic, or a fact from the literature. Concretely, for each source you plan to
keep, open the paper itself, confirm the author, year, and venue match, and confirm
the sentence you attribute to it is a sentence it actually contains. A source that
survives that read is verified; one that cannot be opened, or that does not say what
you claimed, is cut. For the gap itself, add a second, independent method:
**peer reasoning**, walking a classmate through why the map stays quiet where you
placed your gap and letting their one question expose what your retrieval still
owes.

## Project transfer

This chapter is the engine of **Milestone 2, your Verified Evidence and
Contribution Map**, presented as a gallery walk at the Friday studio. M2 asks for a
ledger of at least six sources you personally retrieved, an evidence map with your
contradictions drawn rather than hidden, your gap placed where the map is
demonstrably open, and a bounded contribution line of the form "based on the
sources I retrieved and verified, ___ has not been established for ___." The
retrieval-verification loop you practice here is exactly what fills that ledger, and
the verified map becomes the ground the M3 research charter stands on.

## Defend your decision

In a short paragraph, state the decision this chapter equips and defend it for your
own project. Name one source you have verified and the exact claim it makes. State
your provisional gap in one bounded sentence. Then give its **boundary and
uncertainty**: which compass position it sits in (description or generalization),
how many sources you have actually retrieved so far, and whether your gap is
confirmed by retrieval or is still an unfinished search you must keep chasing before
Friday.
